full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Famous Italian Renaissance artist, Michelangelo, shared, "To touch is to give life."<|12.00|> <|12.00|> Called the divine one by his compatriots, Michelangelo is renowned for his magnificent sculptures, poetry, paintings, and architecture.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I do not believe I have ever seen his work in person, but the painting by Michelangelo,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> The Creation of Adam, has long lived rent free in my mind as they say these days.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Located on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in Vatican City in Rome, it depicts God reaching<|21.00|> <|21.00|> out from the heavens toward a rather comfortably looking Adam.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Their arms extended at length toward each other, fingers nearly touching.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> This painting, along with the whole of his other works on that amazing ceiling,<|15.00|> <|15.00|> took four years to complete, which I imagine was quite the physical, emotional, and spiritual exercise<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> wielding that paintbrush and looking up, looking up.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> Touch has profound implications for what it means to live in this world.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> It is ubiquitous. Everything in creation is touching, connected, intertwined.<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> From the air we breathe, the plants growing in our soil, the water rushing over a rock<|7.62|> <|7.62|> and fire consuming timber, we feel touch, we experience touch, we derive meaning from<|15.58|> <|15.58|> its great power.<|16.64|> <|18.04|> It is no secret to you all then that touch is essential and we benefit from its truth.<|24.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.28|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Human beings depend on and evolve through touch.<|6.30|> <|6.30|> Parents nurture us in our youth.<|10.80|> <|10.80|> Yowch! Don't touch that hot stove!<|15.30|> <|15.30|> We high-five a friend for a job well done.<|19.30|> <|19.30|> We hold the hand of a spouse on a walk and say, "I love you."<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All of us here have experiences every day which involve the extraordinary power of touch.<|8.90|> <|10.54|> One moment in my liturgical duties that leaves me in awe each Sunday is the celebration of Holy Communion.<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yes, I do come back to this repeatedly in my sermons. To set the Lord's table, hold the vessels and elements in my hands before receiving the sacramental gift of Christ's body and blood, the bread and the cup.<|21.90|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> closeness and connection with God.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> Also powerfully, equally powerfully, perhaps even more powerfully,<|10.00|> <|10.00|> is to then I will be made well."<|8.18|> <|8.18|> In both of these stories of pleading desperation, there is a miraculous turn of events.<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The poor woman who reaches out is healed of her 12 years of sickness. The presumed dead child of 12 years gets up and begins to walk after her father's appeal.<|14.18|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We know today that sometimes the peace.<|8.92|> <|10.58|> In that, there is a promise inherent,<|12.78|> <|14.14|> God's loving presence always, always in our lives.<|19.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> reaching In large part, I believe it is to show up, to love and serve, to be present with Christ in all places, especially at the margins, with the tired, hungry, the poor, sick, and disenfranchised.<|20.74|> diocese had reached out to him and helped him get back on his<|17.94|> <|17.94|> feet, find housing and a stable job.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He said this was the
